news_summary: Innovative initiatives are advancing human rights by combating hunger, promoting scientific representation in government, and supporting young women's access to sustainable menstrual products.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives novatrices font progresser les droits humains en luttant contre la faim, en favorisant la représentation des scientifiques au sein du gouvernement et en aidant les jeunes femmes à accéder à des produits d'hygiène menstruelle durables.
news_summary_es: Hay iniciativas innovadoras que contribuyen al avance de los derechos humanos mediante la lucha contra el hambre, el fomento de la representación científica en el gobierno y el apoyo al acceso de las jóvenes a productos menstruales sostenibles.

articles.title: New food alert platform helps humanitarians combat hunger
articles.summary: The UN World Food Programme (WFP) released its next-generation platform on Thursday known as HungerMap Live, a digital monitoring and intelligence site that integrates food security data with predictive modelling to help fight hunger in more than 50 countries.
articles.content:
Tweet URL

Bringing together data from more than 300 analysts and dozens of trusted partners – including government-validated statistics, hunger classification index the IPC, agricultural and economic data – the map provides the most complete and up-to-date picture of hunger facing the world’s most vulnerable.

“It allows you, journalists, but also policymakers, students here in the room, to have your finger on the pulse of global food insecurity,” the WFP’s Director of Food Security and Nutrition Analysis, Jean Martin Bauer, told journalists at the UN Headquarters in New York.

The release of the platform comes at a critical time with limited funding for humanitarian action and the number of people facing the most severe form of hunger has increased 15-fold from 85,000 in 2019, to 1.4 million in 2025, according to the internationally-recognised hunger index, the IPC – which issues famine alerts.

Cost saving potential

Through predictive modelling, the map answers three critical questions: What is the current state of food security across the world? Which countries and regions require urgent attention? And what are the underlying factors contributing to food security needs?

Studies have shown that early warning of emerging food security issues can lead to tremendous cost savings and operational efficiencies. For every dollar invested in WFP’s anticipatory action programs, a minimum of seven dollars is procured in savings.

“Without data, the fight against hunger is fought in the dark,” WFP Executive Director, Cindy McCain, said, “this platform changes that...we’re able to track and predict where, how and why hunger is growing, which means that we don’t just respond to hunger – we get ahead of it.”

AI-assisted forecasting

The map offers AI-assisted forecasting capabilities for projected food needs in WFP-designated Hunger Hotspots, 16 countries with populations already struggling with catastrophic hunger.

Another novel feature is the inclusion of “micronutrient intake adequacy,” which links food-security conditions with the nutritional quality of diets.

This nutrition analysis, developed with support from the Gates Foundation, helps identify populations at risk of hidden hunger caused by inadequate intake of essential vitamins and minerals.

Stopping hunger in its tracks

Funding for global food security monitoring and analysis has been on an alarming decline with WFP's data footprint shrinking 25 percent in the past year.

“You can’t stop hunger if you can’t see it coming,” Mr. Bauer added.

“That’s why it’s crucial that we keep funding the collection of this data, so that society has a trusted, evidence-based early warning system that can alert the world about emerging and alarming conditions, and the risk of human suffering, before it’s too late.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes the launch of HungerMap Live, a next-generation digital platform by the UN World Food Programme that integrates extensive data and AI-assisted forecasting to monitor and predict global food insecurity. This tool enables early warning and anticipatory action to combat hunger in over 50 countries, potentially saving lives and resources on a large scale. It addresses a critical humanitarian issue with broad significance and provides detailed context on its features and impact, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: UN launches HungerMap Live to track and predict global hunger
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Hunger is a growing problem affecting millions worldwide. The UN World Food Programme (WFP) works to fight hunger but needs accurate information to act quickly and effectively.

**What happened** – On Thursday, the WFP introduced HungerMap Live, a new digital platform that collects and combines food security data from over 300 experts and many partners. It uses smart technology, including artificial intelligence, to show where hunger is most severe and predict where it might get worse. The map covers more than 50 countries and includes information about food availability, nutrition, and economic factors.

**Impact** – HungerMap Live helps governments, aid workers, and journalists see the latest and most complete picture of hunger. It can warn about food shortages before they become emergencies, allowing help to arrive sooner. For example, the platform highlights 16 countries with the worst hunger problems and tracks important nutrition details like vitamin and mineral shortages. Early warnings like these can save money and lives by preventing crises instead of just reacting to them.

**What's next step** – The WFP hopes that more funding will support HungerMap Live so it can keep gathering and updating data. This will help the world respond faster to hunger problems and improve the health of vulnerable populations. Continued use of this platform may also encourage new ways to predict and stop hunger before it spreads.

**One-sentence takeaway** – HungerMap Live is a powerful new tool that uses data and technology to spot and prevent hunger crises around the world before they grow worse.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU lance HungerMap Live pour suivre et prédire la faim dans le monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La faim est un problème croissant qui touche des millions de personnes dans le monde. Le Programme alimentaire mondial (PAM) de l’ONU lutte contre la faim mais a besoin d’informations précises pour agir vite et efficacement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jeudi, le PAM a présenté HungerMap Live, une nouvelle plateforme numérique qui rassemble et combine des données sur la sécurité alimentaire provenant de plus de 300 experts et de nombreux partenaires. Elle utilise des technologies intelligentes, dont l’intelligence artificielle, pour montrer où la faim est la plus grave et prédire où elle pourrait s’aggraver. La carte couvre plus de 50 pays et inclut des informations sur la disponibilité des aliments, la nutrition et les facteurs économiques.

**Impact** – HungerMap Live aide les gouvernements, les travailleurs humanitaires et les journalistes à voir le tableau le plus récent et complet de la faim. Elle peut alerter sur les pénuries alimentaires avant qu’elles ne deviennent des urgences, permettant ainsi une aide plus rapide. Par exemple, la plateforme met en lumière 16 pays souffrant le plus de la faim et suit des détails importants sur la nutrition comme les carences en vitamines et minéraux. Ces alertes précoces peuvent économiser de l’argent et des vies en évitant des crises au lieu de simplement y répondre.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le PAM espère obtenir plus de financements pour soutenir HungerMap Live afin qu’elle puisse continuer à collecter et mettre à jour les données. Cela aidera le monde à réagir plus vite aux problèmes de faim et à améliorer la santé des populations vulnérables. L’utilisation continue de cette plateforme pourrait aussi encourager de nouvelles méthodes pour prédire et stopper la faim avant qu’elle ne se répande.

**Phrase clé** – HungerMap Live est un nouvel outil puissant qui utilise les données et la technologie pour repérer et prévenir les crises alimentaires dans le monde avant qu’elles ne s’aggravent.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU lanza HungerMap Live para rastrear y predecir el hambre global
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El hambre es un problema creciente que afecta a millones en todo el mundo. El Programa Mundial de Alimentos (PMA) de la ONU trabaja para combatir el hambre, pero necesita información precisa para actuar rápido y eficazmente.

**Qué pasó** – El jueves, el PMA presentó HungerMap Live, una nueva plataforma digital que reúne y combina datos sobre seguridad alimentaria de más de 300 expertos y muchos socios. Usa tecnología inteligente, incluida la inteligencia artificial, para mostrar dónde el hambre es más grave y predecir dónde podría empeorar. El mapa cubre más de 50 países e incluye información sobre la disponibilidad de alimentos, nutrición y factores económicos.

**Impacto** – HungerMap Live ayuda a gobiernos, trabajadores humanitarios y periodistas a ver la imagen más completa y actualizada del hambre. Puede alertar sobre la escasez de alimentos antes de que se convierta en emergencia, permitiendo que la ayuda llegue más rápido. Por ejemplo, la plataforma destaca 16 países con los peores problemas de hambre y rastrea detalles importantes sobre nutrición como deficiencias de vitaminas y minerales. Estas alertas tempranas pueden ahorrar dinero y vidas al prevenir crisis en lugar de solo reaccionar a ellas.

**Próximo paso** – El PMA espera obtener más fondos para apoyar HungerMap Live y que pueda seguir recolectando y actualizando datos. Esto ayudará al mundo a responder más rápido a los problemas de hambre y mejorar la salud de las poblaciones vulnerables. El uso continuo de esta plataforma también podría fomentar nuevas formas de predecir y detener el hambre antes de que se extienda.

**Frase clave** – HungerMap Live es una herramienta poderosa que usa datos y tecnología para detectar y prevenir crisis de hambre en el mundo antes de que empeoren.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricately layered world map composed of interconnected data points and flowing lines, with symbolic icons representing grains, measuring scales, and nutrient molecules subtly integrated into the map’s contours, all set against a soft, natural background of earth and sky hues, evoking a sense of insight, hope, and proactive global care.

articles.title: ‘Science needs defending’: record number of researchers run for office in US mid-terms
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01134-2Many Democrats making the switch to politics are motivated by the Trump administration’s cuts to science — whereas energy and AI are a pull for some Republicans.
articles.content:
Microbiologist Jasmine Clark is running to become a Democratic representative of Georgia’s 13th district.Credit: Alex Slitz/AP Photo/Alamy

An unprecedented number of US scientists are trading in their lab coats to run for office in the US mid-term elections in November. Many running as Democrats are motivated by the actions of President Donald Trump and his Republican allies to delay and terminate research funding and redefine the goals of government-funded science. Different issues tend to galvanize the scientists running as Republicans, including the artificial-intelligence driven demand for more energy — along with a desire to play a part in science-backed solutions.

The organization 314 Action, which recruits and backs Democratic scientists, engineers and health-care specialists to run for office, has received more than 700 applications from potential candidates seeking support during this election cycle — nearly triple its usual volume (see ‘Political push’). The organization funds candidates to win seats in their state and federal legislatures, with the aim of building “pro-science power at the ballot box”.

Source: 314 Action

For decades, the prevailing wisdom among researchers was that science should be separate from politics. The current political reshaping of US science should drive scientists to realize that this idea “is really a failed business model”, says Shaughnessy Naughton, president of 314 Action, which is based in Washington DC and named after the first three digits of π.

“We scientists are used to sticking to our knitting,” says Sam Wang, a neuroscientist at Princeton University in New Jersey who is running to become a Democratic member of the US House of Representatives for the state’s 12th district. “But I began realizing that science needed defending.”

Running motivations

Wang is one of the candidates who decided to run because of the Trump administration’s actions. He watched last year as his colleagues’ research grants were abruptly cancelled or delayed, and as trainees, such as postdoctoral fellows, had to be let go because of a lack of funding.

‘Shattered’: US scientists speak out about how Trump policies disrupted their careers

He likens the frantic process of establishing a political campaign to setting up a research laboratory — only ten times faster. And he’s hoping to bring some of his knowledge from the lab into politics, by applying the scientific method to what he calls a broken system. In 2024, he used statistics to argue before a federal court that the design of some of New Jersey’s election ballots was giving an unfair advantage to certain candidates. (The federal judge later struck down the design.) If he wins during the mid-terms, Wang says, he’ll continue using evidence and data to fight for fairness.

Jasmine Clark, a microbiologist at Emory University in Atlanta, Georgia, already serves on her state’s legislature, but the reshaping of US science over the past year has motivated her to run for an even higher political office in 2026. She is now campaigning to become a Democratic congressional representative for Georgia’s 13th district.

During the year after Trump took office, some 10,000 PhD-level scientists left the US government as, in an effort to downsize the federal workforce, the administration fired them or encouraged them to retire early. One US agency that lost many scientists was the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), headquartered in Clark’s home state of Georgia. Health secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr, who has called the agency corrupt, drove much of that exodus while also taking actions that many scientists oppose, such as changing vaccine recommendations without consulting specialists.

In response, Clark wants to bring data and science to the policymaking table. “We need people who actually care about the truth sitting in these seats.”

A science wave?

The number of people with a scientific background running for office has historically been very low, says Kristoffer Shields, a political scientist at Rutgers University in New Brunswick, New Jersey.

Trump’s new science advisers include 12 technology chiefs — and one academic

His team found in 2025 that only about 3% of the seats in state legislatures across the country are filled by people with training in science, engineering or health care. But with issues such as climate change, artificial intelligence and public health dominating legislative agendas, Shields expects this number to grow.

One hot-button topic for which scientific expertise is useful is energy, says Jeff Wilson, a retired nuclear engineer and US Navy veteran who is running to become a Republican representative of Illinois’s 13th district. Wilson wants to ensure that the United States becomes energy-independent. If elected, he says, he will help to build more nuclear power plants in the state. His district “needs someone that’s articulate, scientific-minded, practical-minded”, and “that can provide common-sense solutions, particularly with energy”, he adds.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and inspiring trend of scientists running for political office to defend and promote science-based policymaking, which has broad societal implications for evidence-based governance, public health, and energy policy. This shift represents a meaningful positive change in political representation that can benefit society at large.
articles.personality_title: Record number of US scientists run for office to defend and promote science
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the United States, fewer than 3% of state lawmakers have backgrounds in science, engineering, or health care. Many scientists have traditionally stayed out of politics, believing science and politics should be separate. However, recent government actions that cut science funding and changed health policies have motivated more scientists to become involved.

**What happened** – Ahead of the November 2026 mid-term elections, a record number of scientists are running for political office in the US. Many Democrats are motivated by past government cuts to research funding under President Donald Trump’s administration. On the Republican side, candidates are often driven by issues like energy independence and the impact of artificial intelligence. Organizations like 314 Action have seen a huge rise in scientists applying for support, receiving over 700 applications this election cycle, nearly triple their usual number.

**Impact** – This increase in scientists running for office is important because it means more lawmakers will have direct experience with science and data. Candidates like Jasmine Clark and Sam Wang want to bring scientific thinking into government decisions, especially on topics like public health, energy, and fair elections. This shift could lead to policies that rely more on facts and evidence, improving how challenges like climate change and health crises are handled.

**What's next step** – If these candidates win their elections, they will join state and federal legislatures with a stronger focus on science-based policy. Groups like 314 Action plan to keep recruiting scientists to run in future elections, aiming to build lasting “pro-science power” in government. This trend may encourage more scientists to see politics as a place where they can protect and advance research and public well-being.

**One-sentence takeaway** – More US scientists are running for office than ever before to bring scientific knowledge and evidence into government decisions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nombre record de scientifiques américains se présentent aux élections pour défendre la science
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aux États-Unis, moins de 3 % des élus des assemblées d’État ont une formation en sciences, ingénierie ou santé. Beaucoup de scientifiques évitaient la politique, pensant que science et politique devaient rester séparées. Mais les récentes coupes dans le financement de la recherche et les changements dans les politiques de santé ont poussé davantage de scientifiques à s’engager.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Avant les élections de mi-mandat de novembre 2026, un nombre record de scientifiques se présentent aux élections aux États-Unis. Beaucoup de démocrates sont motivés par les coupes dans la recherche sous l’administration Trump. Chez les républicains, les candidats sont souvent attirés par des sujets comme l’indépendance énergétique et l’intelligence artificielle. L’organisation 314 Action a reçu plus de 700 candidatures cette année, soit presque trois fois plus que d’habitude.

**Impact** – Cette augmentation est importante car elle signifie que plus de législateurs auront une expérience directe des sciences et des données. Des candidats comme Jasmine Clark et Sam Wang veulent appliquer la pensée scientifique dans les décisions gouvernementales, notamment sur la santé publique, l’énergie et l’équité des élections. Ce changement pourrait conduire à des politiques plus basées sur les faits et les preuves.

**Prochaine étape** – Si ces candidats sont élus, ils renforceront la présence de la science dans les législatures d’État et fédérales. Des groupes comme 314 Action veulent continuer à encourager les scientifiques à se présenter aux élections, pour construire un pouvoir politique « pro-science » durable. Cette tendance pourrait inciter plus de scientifiques à voir la politique comme un moyen de protéger la recherche et le bien-être public.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nombre record de scientifiques américains se présentent aux élections pour intégrer la science et les preuves dans les décisions politiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Número récord de científicos estadounidenses se postulan para defender y promover la ciencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Estados Unidos, menos del 3 % de los legisladores estatales tienen formación en ciencias, ingeniería o salud. Muchos científicos solían evitar la política, pensando que la ciencia y la política debían mantenerse separadas. Pero los recientes recortes en la financiación de la investigación y cambios en las políticas de salud han motivado a más científicos a involucrarse.

**Qué pasó** – Antes de las elecciones de medio término en noviembre de 2026, un número récord de científicos se postulan para cargos políticos en EE. UU. Muchos demócratas están motivados por los recortes a la investigación bajo la administración Trump. Del lado republicano, los candidatos se interesan en temas como la independencia energética y la inteligencia artificial. La organización 314 Action ha recibido más de 700 solicitudes este ciclo electoral, casi el triple de lo habitual.

**Impacto** – Este aumento es importante porque significa que más legisladores tendrán experiencia directa en ciencia y datos. Candidatos como Jasmine Clark y Sam Wang quieren llevar el pensamiento científico a las decisiones del gobierno, especialmente en salud pública, energía y elecciones justas. Este cambio podría llevar a políticas basadas más en hechos y evidencias.

**Próximo paso** – Si estos candidatos ganan, fortalecerán el enfoque en la ciencia en las legislaturas estatales y federales. Organizaciones como 314 Action planean seguir reclutando científicos para futuras elecciones, buscando construir un poder «pro-ciencia» duradero en el gobierno. Esta tendencia puede motivar a más científicos a ver la política como un lugar para proteger y avanzar la investigación y el bienestar público.

**Resumen en una frase** – Más científicos estadounidenses se postulan que nunca para llevar el conocimiento científico y la evidencia a las decisiones gubernamentales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse group of symbolic laboratory glassware—flasks, test tubes, and microscopes—transformed into vibrant campaign signs and ballot boxes set against a softly glowing backdrop of a stylized U.S. map, with subtle motifs of DNA strands and circuit patterns weaving through, representing scientists stepping into politics to defend and advance evidence-based governance.

articles.title: France to reimburse young women for reusable menstrual products
articles.summary: France announced Tuesday that it will expand access to sustainable menstrual products by reimbursing reusable cups and underwear for eligible young women and low-income groups. The move, aimed at tackling period poverty, is expected to benefit millions once implemented through the country’s social security system from the upcoming academic year.
articles.content:
This photograph taken on April 4, 2023, shows a Luneale menstrual cup, in Courseulles-sur-Mer, northwestern France.

France's social security is to reimburse the cost of reusable menstrual cups and underwear for women under the age of 26 or battling poverty, the government said Thursday.

The move to tackle period poverty is expected to help 6.7 million people – almost a tenth of France's population of 69 million – from the start of the next academic year in the autumn, it says.

Women under 26 with a state health insurance card, as well as women of all ages who benefit from special healthcare support due to their limited income, will be able to get their money back after buying these products in a pharmacy.

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again 01:29

Parliament approved the measure as part of the country's social security budget for 2024.

But there was no decree to order implementation, causing anger among feminist groups and companies making the sustainable sanitary items.

A survey of 4,000 women in France in November showed one in ten used alternatives to mainstream period products such as ripped up clothes due to tight budgets, according to French charity Dons Solidaires.

France in 2016 reduced sales tax on period products from 20 per cent to 5.5 per cent.

In 2020, Scotland became the first country in the world to sign into law free universal access to period products in public buildings.

(FRANCE 24 with AFP)
articles.source: Health: news, videos, reports and analysis - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant government policy in France to reimburse reusable menstrual products for young women and low-income groups, directly addressing period poverty. This policy has broad social impact, benefiting millions and promoting sustainable health and economic equity. It is a focused, substantive story on a meaningful human rights and public health issue.
articles.personality_title: France to reimburse reusable menstrual products for young women and low-income groups
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Period poverty means some women cannot afford the products they need during their menstrual cycle. France has been working to make menstrual products more affordable, including lowering taxes on these products in 2016.

**What happened** – France announced that starting next school year, women under 26 and those with low incomes can get reimbursed for buying reusable menstrual cups and underwear. This will happen through the country’s social security system. The government expects this to help about 6.7 million people.

**Impact** – This is important because it helps young and low-income women access sustainable menstrual products without worrying about the cost. Using reusable products also supports the environment by reducing waste. This policy is one of the few that focuses on both health and sustainability.

**What's next step** – The government will implement this reimbursement through pharmacies and social security from the autumn academic year. Feminist groups and companies making these products are waiting for official rules to start the process smoothly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – France will help millions of young and low-income women afford reusable menstrual products by offering reimbursements through social security starting next school year.
articles.personality_title_fr: La France rembourse les produits menstruels réutilisables pour les jeunes femmes et les personnes à faibles revenus
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La précarité menstruelle signifie que certaines femmes ne peuvent pas se permettre les produits nécessaires pendant leurs règles. La France a déjà réduit les taxes sur ces produits en 2016 pour les rendre plus abordables.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La France a annoncé que, dès la prochaine rentrée scolaire, les femmes de moins de 26 ans et celles à faibles revenus pourront se faire rembourser les coupes menstruelles et sous-vêtements réutilisables. Ce remboursement se fera via la sécurité sociale. Le gouvernement estime que cela aidera environ 6,7 millions de personnes.

**Impact** – Cette mesure est importante car elle permet aux jeunes femmes et aux personnes à faibles revenus d’accéder à des produits menstruels durables sans se soucier du coût. L’utilisation de produits réutilisables aide aussi à protéger l’environnement en réduisant les déchets. Cette politique combine santé et écologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement mettra en place ce remboursement dans les pharmacies à partir de la rentrée scolaire d’automne. Les groupes féministes et les fabricants attendent les règles officielles pour que tout se déroule bien.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La France aidera des millions de jeunes femmes et personnes à faibles revenus à acheter des produits menstruels réutilisables en les remboursant via la sécurité sociale dès la prochaine rentrée scolaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Francia reembolsa productos menstruales reutilizables para jóvenes y personas con bajos ingresos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La pobreza menstrual significa que algunas mujeres no pueden pagar los productos que necesitan durante su menstruación. Francia ya había reducido los impuestos sobre estos productos en 2016 para hacerlos más accesibles.

**Qué pasó** – Francia anunció que a partir del próximo año escolar, las mujeres menores de 26 años y las que tienen bajos ingresos podrán recibir un reembolso por comprar copas menstruales y ropa interior reutilizable. Esto se hará a través del sistema de seguridad social. Se espera que beneficie a unos 6,7 millones de personas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ayuda a las jóvenes y a las personas con bajos ingresos a acceder a productos menstruales sostenibles sin preocuparse por el costo. Usar productos reutilizables también ayuda al medio ambiente al reducir la basura. Esta política combina salud y sostenibilidad.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno implementará este reembolso en farmacias a partir del inicio del año escolar en otoño. Los grupos feministas y las empresas que fabrican estos productos esperan las reglas oficiales para comenzar sin problemas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Francia ayudará a millones de jóvenes y personas con bajos ingresos a comprar productos menstruales reutilizables ofreciendo reembolsos a través de la seguridad social desde el próximo año escolar.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a softly glowing menstrual cup and reusable cloth underwear resting gently on a wooden pharmacy counter, surrounded by delicate wildflowers and soft natural light filtering through a nearby window, symbolizing care, accessibility, and support for young women’s health in France.
